titleStmt.title: Canto 3: malarvaṉampukkakātai
The Canto in which [Maṇimēkalai and Cutamati] Enter the Flower Grove (171 lines)

summary.p: 3.1-10 (10 lines) Maṇimēkalai has an understanding of Cause and Effect when she hears the story of her parents' suffering. She weeps, and her tears wet her garland.
3.11-15 (5 lines) Mātavi sees that Maṇimēkalai's tears have spoilt her garland, and sends her to fetch new flowers to make a new one.
3.16-25 (10 lines) Cutamati intervenes: Maṇimēkalai should not go alone, since her beauty while weeping will attract the unwanted attentions of men.
2.37-41 Mātavi replies that since losing her lover Kōvalaṉ she has renounced shame.
2.42-48 Mātavi lists the options open to widows: spontaneous death, the pyre, or penance.
2.49-55b Mātavi cites Kaṇṇaki as a paragon of female virtue.
2.55c -57 Mātavi states her wish that Maṇimēkalai, who is Kaṇṇaki's daughter (by marriage), renounce the courtesan life and become a nun.
2.58-69 Mātavi recounts how, after the death of Kōvalaṉ, she went to a Buddhist teacher, Aṟavaṇa for instruction.

2.70-75 Mātavi concludes her speech to Vayantamālai with a request to report her decision to Cittirāpati and the other women. Having failed to convince Mātavi, Vayantamālai returns in despair to Cittirāpati.

div.p: The third song, in which [Maṇimēkalai and Cutamati] enter the flower grove.

lg.l: with which Matavi had spoken to Vayantamālai
became apparent [to Maṇimēkalai] during [Mātavi's] speech about [her] grief.
The reality of Cause and Effect confronted
Maṇimēkalai like the scent of huge flowers.
Pain burnt the inside of her ears with the [same] hot, miserable agony
in which [her] father and mother had suffered so much.
Love was stirred up in the girl's heart,
destroying [her] beauty with streaks in [her] beautiful, red eyes.
Sending tears of sorrow rolling down [her cheeks],
[love] poured tears onto [her] garland of shining petals
and fragrant blossoms opening their buds
and wiping away [her] tears with [her] lovely red hand,
like a lotus joining the cool moon,
said, 'The garland of pure nature has lots its pure fitness.
[Go and] fetch shining blossoms [to make a new garland]!'
who was weaving big flowers [into a garland] with the girl whose hair was coiled with nectar-[filled] blossoms (Maṇimēkalai),
[over-]heard [what Mātavi said to Maṇimēkalai] and began to speak sorrowfully:
'If Kāmadeva sees the tears shed from the rims of the sapphire[-like] eyes
to rival the choice blossoms on an ornamental gliterring blue lotus—
as she suffers unappeasable grief over and over again,
listenting to the great misfortune suffered by [her] respected parents,
he will lay down [his] bow and tremble.
And if men see the maiden, will they [ever] leave [her alone]?
Would they not [have to] be hermaphrodites, if—because of [their] character—they remained unmoved?
Moreover, listen, O [lady of] choice jewels,
to the reason why I came to this city here!
I am the only daughter of a twice-born
called Kaucikaṉ from [the town of] Caṇpai of the Vēḷāḷa-s,
where much wealth of the sea grows.
Not being afraid all on my own, with reckless heart,
I was plucking blossoms in the pleasure-garden,
who had come to see the great festival
put on for the King of the Gods (Indra) in the ancient town beloved of Śrī (Kāvirippūmpaṭṭiṉam),
wearing a flower garland, a necklace and golden ornaments,
having a form worshipped by many [but] invisible to mortals,
picked me up and taking me [with him], rose up into the sky.
He made me stay there. I became his property.
Then he abandoned me here and was gone in the blink of an eye, although his place is far away.
As for Maṇimēkalai—that sapphire-[coloured] flowering branch—
she is not the sort of girl who should pluck blossoms on her own.
If [we] go to the water-tank behind the fortress—
with its arbours of good trees heaped with many blossoms—
there will be many people there in the entourage of/close to the King of the World (the Cōḻa King).
Except for the celestials, no humans will want
to go to the uyyāṉa garden
during the fair days on which the festival for the King of the Celestials (Indra) is being held,
that the bhūta [who dwells in the garden], who carries a noose in his hand, guards
every one of the many trees—on which the bees do not hum songs,
because they are hung with great garlands of blossoms which never fade.
to the Campāti Grove, where Campāti used to dwell—
he who lost his unfurling wings because of the heat of the hot rays [of the sun];
nor to the Kavēra Grove, where used to dwell Kavēra–
the father of Lady Kāvērī whose waters never run dry–
– since they know that [these two groves] are under the binding[-power]
of the ancient old aṇaṅku-s, who attack [intruders].
where many trees bloom by edict of the Holy One (the Buddha)
of unending penance and a great resolution
to protect precious lives with grace and love.
